title: First Order Linear Differential Equations
subtitle: The core solving toolkit: separation of variables, integrating factors, initial value problems, substitutions, and first physical models.
description: The core solving toolkit: separation of variables, integrating factors, initial value problems, substitutions, and first physical models.

atAGlance.title: Unit 2 at a Glance: Core Sub-Topics
atAGlance.summary: This unit assembles the core solving toolkit of first order theory. Each sub-topic below builds on the last, moving from raw integration techniques toward genuine physical models.

bullets.body: splitting a rate law into a pure $x$ side and a pure $y$ side, then integrating each independently.

bullets.lead: First-Order Linear Equations and Integrating Factors
bullets.body: the standard form $y' + P(x)y = Q(x)$ and the factor that collapses the left side into a single derivative.
using a known data point to pin the arbitrary constant and select one curve from the solution family.
the substitution $y = vx$ that reduces a degree-matched equation to a separable one.

bullets.lead: Applications: Growth, Decay, and Newton's Law of Cooling
bullets.body: the exponential models that these methods unlock.

modules.title: Separable Equations
modules.topics: A first order equation is \emph{separable} when its right side factors into a function of $x$ alone times a function of $y$ alone --- that single structural test is the whole game. Once it factors, you divide and multiply until every $y$ rides with $dy$ and every $x$ rides with $dx$, then integrate both sides.

formulas.title: Standard Form and Method: Separable
formulas.body:
A separable equation and its solution scheme:
\[
    \frac{dy}{dx} = g(x)\,h(y)
    \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
    \int \frac{1}{h(y)}\,dy = \int g(x)\,dx + C.
\]
One constant $C$ suffices, since the two constants of integration merge into
a single arbitrary constant.

examples.title: Worked Example 2.1: Solve $\frac{dy}{dx} = 6x^{2}y$
examples.setup:
\strong{Step 1.} Separate the variables:
\[ \frac{1}{y}\,dy = 6x^{2}\,dx. \]
\strong{Step 2.} Integrate both sides:
\[ \ln|y| = 2x^{3} + C. \]
\strong{Step 3.} Exponentiate to isolate $y$:
\[ y = e^{2x^{3} + C} = C\,e^{2x^{3}}. \]
The dropped solution $y = 0$ is recovered as the case $C = 0$.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Lost Equilibrium Solutions
warning.body:
Dividing by $h(y)$ silently assumes $h(y) \neq 0$. Any constant value of $y$ that
makes $h(y) = 0$ is an equilibrium solution that this step can quietly discard ---
always check it separately.

modules.title: First-Order Linear Equations and Integrating Factors
modules.topics: A first order equation is \emph{linear} when $y$ and $y'$ each appear only to the first power and never multiply one another. Before reading off anything, you must normalize so the derivative carries coefficient one --- only then is $P(x)$ truly the coefficient of $y$.

formulas.title: Standard Form and Integrating Factor
formulas.body:
Standard form, integrating factor, and solution:
\[
    \frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)\,y = Q(x),
    \qquad
    \mu(x) = e^{\int P(x)\,dx},
\]
\[
    \frac{d}{dx}\big[\mu(x)\,y\big] = \mu(x)\,Q(x)
    \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
    y = \frac{1}{\mu(x)}\left( \int \mu(x)\,Q(x)\,dx + C \right).
\]

methodology.title: Algorithm: Solving a First-Order Linear Equation
steps: Write the equation in standard form $y' + P(x)\,y = Q(x)$, dividing through so the derivative carries coefficient one.
Compute the integrating factor $\mu(x) = e^{\int P(x)\,dx}$.
Multiply through by $\mu(x)$; the left side collapses to the single derivative $\frac{d}{dx}\big[\mu(x)\,y\big]$.
Integrate both sides, then divide by $\mu(x)$ to isolate $y$.

examples.title: Worked Example 2.2: Solve $\frac{dy}{dx} + y = e^{x}$
examples.setup:
\strong{Step 1.} Read off $P(x) = 1$, so the integrating factor is
\[ \mu = e^{\int 1\,dx} = e^{x}. \]
\strong{Step 2.} Multiply through; the left side collapses to a derivative:
\[ \frac{d}{dx}\big[e^{x}y\big] = e^{x}\cdot e^{x} = e^{2x}. \]
\strong{Step 3.} Integrate both sides:
\[ e^{x}y = \frac{1}{2}e^{2x} + C. \]
\strong{Step 4.} Divide by $e^{x}$:
\[ y = \frac{1}{2}e^{x} + C e^{-x}. \]

modules.title: Initial Value Problems
modules.topics: The general solution of a first order equation is a whole family of curves indexed by one arbitrary constant. An \emph{initial condition} $y(x_0) = y_0$ selects exactly one member --- it fixes the constant to the value that forces the curve through the given point.

formulas.title: General versus Particular Solution
formulas.body:
A first order equation needs exactly \strong{one} initial condition. The
general solution carries the free constant; the particular solution has it
determined:
\[
    y = F(x) + C \;\;\xrightarrow{\;y(x_0)=y_0\;}\;\; C = y_0 - F(x_0).
\]

examples.title: Worked Example 2.3: Solve $\frac{dy}{dx} = 3x^{2}$, $\;y(1)=5$
examples.setup:
\strong{Step 1.} Integrate for the general solution:
\[ y = x^{3} + C. \]
\strong{Step 2.} Apply $y(1) = 5$:
\[ 5 = (1)^{3} + C \;\Longrightarrow\; C = 4. \]
\strong{Step 3.} State the particular solution:
\[ y = x^{3} + 4. \]

modules.title: Homogeneous First-Order Equations and Substitution
modules.topics: A function $f(x,y)$ is \emph{homogeneous of degree $n$} when scaling both inputs pulls out a clean power of the scale factor: $f(tx, ty) = t^{n}f(x,y)$. An equation $M\,dx + N\,dy = 0$ is homogeneous when $M$ and $N$ share the same degree --- and that shared degree is precisely what lets the ratio substitution work.

formulas.title: The Substitution $y = vx$
formulas.body:
For a homogeneous equation, set
\[
    y = vx, \qquad \frac{dy}{dx} = v + x\frac{dv}{dx},
\]
which always reduces the equation to a \emph{separable} one in $v$ and $x$.
After solving for $v$, back-substitute $v = \dfrac{y}{x}$.

examples.title: Worked Example 2.4: Solve $\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{x + 2y}{x}$
examples.setup:
\strong{Step 1.} Rewrite the right side and substitute $y = vx$:
\[ \frac{dy}{dx} = 1 + 2\frac{y}{x} = 1 + 2v, \qquad v + x\frac{dv}{dx} = 1 + 2v. \]
\strong{Step 2.} Cancel $v$ and separate:
\[ x\frac{dv}{dx} = 1 + v \;\Longrightarrow\; \frac{1}{1+v}\,dv = \frac{1}{x}\,dx. \]
\strong{Step 3.} Integrate and exponentiate:
\[ \ln|1+v| = \ln|x| + C \;\Longrightarrow\; 1 + v = Cx. \]
\strong{Step 4.} Back-substitute $v = \frac{y}{x}$ and clear:
\[ 1 + \frac{y}{x} = Cx \;\Longrightarrow\; y = Cx^{2} - x. \]

modules.title: Applications: Growth, Decay, and Newton's Law of Cooling
modules.topics: When a rate of change is proportional to the current amount, the solution is exponential --- this single idea drives population models, radioactive decay, and cooling alike.

formulas.title: Exponential Models
formulas.body:
Growth and decay:
\[
    \frac{dP}{dt} = kP \;\Longrightarrow\; P(t) = P_{0}e^{kt}
    \qquad (k>0 \text{ grows},\; k<0 \text{ decays}),
\]
with doubling time $t = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$. Newton's law of cooling:
\[
    \frac{dT}{dt} = -k\,(T - T_{s}) \;\Longrightarrow\;
    T(t) = T_{s} + (T_{0} - T_{s})\,e^{-kt},
\]
so $T \to T_{s}$ as $t \to \infty$.

examples.title: Worked Example 2.5: A culture doubles in 3 hours
examples.setup:
\strong{Step 1.} The model $P = P_{0}e^{kt}$ doubles when $e^{3k} = 2$:
\[ 3k = \ln 2 \;\Longrightarrow\; k = \frac{\ln 2}{3}. \]
\strong{Step 2.} The growth law is therefore
\[ P(t) = P_{0}\,e^{(\ln 2 / 3)\,t} = P_{0}\,2^{\,t/3}. \]
Every $3$ hours the population multiplies by two, as required.

cheat.subtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: First Order Linear Differential Equations

takeaway.title: Unit 2 Key Takeaway
takeaway.body:
Every method in this unit reduces to a single move --- rewrite the equation
into a form you can integrate. \highlight{Separation of variables}
splits a factored rate law; the \highlight{integrating factor
$\mu = e^{\int P\,dx}$} collapses a linear left side into one derivative; and
the \highlight{substitution $y = vx$} turns a homogeneous equation
separable. Read the structure first, and the technique always follows.
